author: @rodrigoanda7845
text:
Hi Rob. I just asked the similar question in the omnidirectional speaker video: could a piezo element  be applied to create an ultrasonic device for cleaning or processing nutrients?. Would it be better to use a speaker or a piezo electric element?
Thank you for all your contributions!!

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: Ultrasonic cleaners use transducers, but I'm not sure if they're piezo or just driven at a certain frequency. That's the main difference - the piezo element contains its own crystal that determines its frequency, and it can only do that frequency. A coil and magnet has to be driven by an external circuit and can make any frequency or combination thereof. I have no idea about processing nutrients, that's something I never heard of. But you can make an ultrasonic device with either method. For the hobbyist tinkerer, a coil and magnet is probably easier, because you don't need an assortment of crystals that you have to change out to test different frequencies.

author: @opera5714
text: Many years ago a company made these fans for cooling circuit boards. They were piezo and the company almost went broke. Then an aroma company discovered them and saved the company by putting them in their aroma dispensers. Back in 64' I tried making a helicopter solid state by creating ionization flows with 14,000VDC after seeing it in Popular Mechanics. It didn't fly, but it made some wind.   When I saw the title I thought you were going to encourage your viewers to plat with high voltage.

replies.author: @tequilaboxer1
replies.text: The helicopter idea is fascinating. Was the airflow somehow directed *im assuming you were using piezo-electric fan concept* if you managed to direct the airflow, what was the yield, what kind of results did you get? Ive worked in hvac for years and its the kind of idea ive been toying with but never succeeded with so far. Directing airflow with these simple fans could be gamechanging in terms of power consumption, and plastic waste problems.

replies.author: @opera5714
replies.text: @tequilaboxer1 The idea is very similar to a furnace electrostatic air cleaner. Pins faced down towards an aluminum screen with the opposite polarity. Charged molecules traveled to the screen, lost their charge and kept going.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Good build.  Also, a salvaged fan (or hard drive or cd drive) motor with an eccentric or a cam lobe would work -- I favor a tri-lobe cam with a reuleaux triangle, for a 3x rpm frequency.  Vibration motors for buzzers are also really cheap.  As for the fan design, it always reminded me of the electric school or fire alarm actuator, with a clapper between two half-spherical bells -- a linear motor with a double throw.

replies.text: The density of water compared with air may damp it dow too much. ..... But it is one Cool Idea!

replies.author: @custos3249
replies.text:
@totherarf Yup, plus the locomotion of fish involves their entire body and other supporting fins, not just their tail flapping about. And there's a reason why snakes aren't great swimmers. They can, but not with all that much speed. Wouldn't work with just one flipper even if sufficient force could be produced. You'd have oscillations in the ship itself to deal with, which is why bacteria that use a single flagellum don't generally whip it back and forth but rotate it in a corkscrew-like pattern. But cilia on the other hand... Then again, ya run into complexity issues there.

Also, if with just two, ya got a bird, which people have already made small working prototypes of. I forget the technical term for that though.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @custos3249 One difference might be that microbes tend to use a circular csa flagellum (I think) You are talking about a 3d object albeit a flat one! Also driving it at a higher resonant frequency may make a difference too. Worth checking it out though!

replies.author: @custos3249
replies.text: @totherarf In the case of cilia, i.e. having an array of fins, a higher frequency may help, but again, complexity issues. With just 1 fin, it'd be like over-volting a motor. Speed would increase, but torque, which is what you need as the volume of water (the load) is the same, wouldn't by much. You could make the fin smaller too, but good luck on getting useful movement out of it. You'd need more current, which brings us back to the snake comparison for locomotion (not form factor though). Technically, we'd be looking at eels and there rhythmic movements. I keep wanted to call it "peristaltic movement" but that's technically about the coordinated movement of throat muscles - the 3D version of what I'm talking about.

replies.author: @nunyabidniz2868
replies.text: Back when the Mac Plus was one of Apple's top of the line offerings, there was a company that came out with a solid-state fan kit for it so that if you maxed out the memory [4mb: woohoo!] you could still keep it running in a warm environment [Mac Plus had convection cooling only.] The big jump to the Mac SE when it superseded the Mac Plus was the inclusion of a fan [to make the available internal hard drive a workable option.] So yeah, I heard about them 30 years ago, but still haven't seen them make it big for some reason...
